data.title: What is oshikatsu? Nearly 50% of Japan’s young workers seek trend for fulfilment
data.description:
Nearly half of Japanese full-time workers in their 20s engage in oshikatsu – a practice that involves devoting time to pop or sports idols, anime characters or hobbies such as collecting model trains – according to a recent recruitment survey.
The findings, released on Friday by Mynavi Corp, indicate that the trend – spanning everything from social media posts to buying merchandise and attending fan events or concerts – has shifted from a niche subculture into the mainstream and could even bolster workplace motivation.
The survey, conducted in October, gathered around 20,000 responses from employees in their 20s to 50s nationwide. While the overall oshikatsu participation rate was 28.5 per cent, it reached 49.2 per cent among workers in their 20s and declined steadily across older age groups.
Pollsters attribute the trend’s rise to the spread of social media and a wider desire among young Japanese workers for more balance in their lives. The term oshikatsu, which translates to “activities that support my favourite”, gained traction online during the pandemic before entering mainstream Japanese usage.


Nearly 80 per cent of participants in the recent survey said oshikatsu brought them fulfilment, and more than 60 per cent said it helped motivate them at work.
The average expenditure by fans on such activities was more than 13,000 yen (US$84) monthly.
In April, Reuters reported that oshikatsu had become a significant driver of consumer spending in Japan, with some 14 million participants contributing an estimated 3.5 trillion yen to the economy each year.
The wave has caught the attention of major corporations eager to cash in on this consumer segment.
Fabio Gygi, a senior lecturer in anthropology at the University of London, noted in a commentary for The Conversation, an academic opinion and analysis platform, that pandemic-era supply chain disruption and geopolitical shocks had “caused Japanese consumers to reduce their spending”.
“However, with wages set to rise again for the third time in three years, the government is cautiously optimistic that economic growth can be rekindled through consumer-driven spending. Entertainment and media companies are looking to oshikatsu as a potential driver of this,” Gygi added.

Inflation proof
In a Japan Times report in September, Kohei Okazaki, chief market economist at Nomura Securities, said the trend was proving resilient even as prices rose, noting that “oshikatsu-related consumption is expanding due to consumers’ desire to give support, regardless of cost.”
The report highlighted apparel retailer Shimamura as one of the companies attracting investor interest for its focus on products featuring popular characters and collaborations with influencers.
Shimamura’s share price this year reached its highest level in more than twenty-five years.
Investors have also flocked to firms with strong character-related intellectual property, including Sanrio – owner of Hello Kitty, My Melody and Kuromi – while Bandai Namco Holdings reported higher revenue and profit from April to June on strong sales tied to the mecha anime series Mobile Suit Gundam.

data.title: 34 arrested in Hong Kong crackdown on illegal workers, including 13 ex-helpers
data.description:
Hong Kong immigration officers have arrested 34 people, including 13 former domestic helpers who overstayed their visas, during an operation to crack down on illegal labour.
The operation is the third in two months in which current and former domestic helpers were caught working illegally.
The Immigration Department said on Friday that officers conducted a three-day anti-illegal work operation from Tuesday to Thursday, carrying out raids on multiple locations across the city, including restaurants and food manufacturing plants.
Officers arrested 34 people, including 24 illegal workers, two overstayers and eight others suspected of employing illegal workers.
The arrested illegal workers included one man and 23 women aged 22 to 52. Among them were 13 former domestic helpers who overstayed their visas, 10 holders of recognisance forms, which prohibited them from taking any employment in the city, and one mainland Chinese visitor who overstayed a visa.
Most of those arrested were engaged in jobs such as dishwashing, cleaning and kitchen work, with the longest stint lasting nearly a year. They earned daily wages of HK$300 (US$38.50) to HK$500.
Two other overstayers arrested were Indonesian women aged 23 and 38.

The remaining eight arrested for allegedly employing illegal workers included seven men and one woman, aged 27 to 64, and they were owners or people in charge of the companies or restaurants involved.
“The Immigration Department will continue to actively follow up on the investigation, and we do not rule out that more people will be arrested or prosecuted in connection with the case,” said Mo Chuen-kit, the department’s deputy commander of the task force.
He reminded employers that all domestic helpers who had been dismissed and whose contracts had been terminated early or had expired were prohibited from working in Hong Kong.
The government amended the Immigration Ordinance in 2021 to increase penalties for employers who hired illegal workers, raising the maximum penalty from a fine of HK$350,000 and imprisonment for three years to a fine of HK$500,000 and 10 years’ imprisonment.

data.title: In Malaysia’s Kedah, Chinese text at factory event sparks double-standard accusation
data.description:
A senior official in Malaysia’s northern state of Kedah has defended the use of Chinese characters on a foundation stone for a tyre plant, after the inscription drew criticism in a country where public displays of Chinese text can become flashpoints over national identity and political loyalty.
The controversy began after photos circulated of a foundation stone bearing Chinese characters at the groundbreaking ceremony for Prinx Tire Malaysia, the local subsidiary of Chinese tyre maker Prinx Chengshan, at Kedah Rubber City (KRC) earlier this week. The 2.6 billion ringgit (US$627 million) plant is set to become the first anchor investment at the 1,244-acre industrial estate.
The photos have prompted a wave of online criticism, with some social media users accusing Kedah’s ruling Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party (PAS) of applying a double standard. Critics noted that leaders from the Islamist party have previously pushed for stricter use of Malay and Jawi – the Arabic-derived script used for writing the Malay language – on commercial signboards.
One widely shared comment on social media read: “Even the foundation stone is in Chinese. PAS claims to uphold Jawi – what a bunch of liars, phui. If this happened in Penang or Selangor, their supporters would be screaming nonstop.”
Defending the move, Haim Hilman Abdullah, the state executive councillor in charge of industry and investment and a PAS central committee member, said in a social media post that the foundation stone also carried Malay text and all official speeches at the ceremony were delivered in Malay.
He urged the public “not to be worried” about the Chinese inscription, which he said simply read “laying the foundation”, and argued that Malaysia should show mutual respect when hosting foreign investors.
“These foreign investors [from China] bring in new technology, job opportunities and revenue for the people. As hosts, we respect their identity, the same way they respect our diverse languages and cultures,” he wrote in his social media post, as quoted by Free Malaysia Today.

He added: “PAS and PN are not static. We have never hindered progress, never restricted anyone’s right to be religious or ethnic. We defend the national identity, but at the same time open the door to billions of ringgit in investments for the future of the people.”
Haim also used the post to appeal to voters in Sabah, saying that if they wanted a “more stable, more developed and more open future”, they should support PAS and the broader Perikatan Nasional coalition.
Prinx’s Kedah facility marks a significant step for KRC, which the federal government launched in 2022 to develop a dedicated rubber-industry hub in Malaysia’s northern region. At its inauguration, then prime minister Ismail Sabri Yaakob said the park was expected to generate around 15,000 jobs and attract 10 billion ringgit in investment.
The language debate echoes a wider national conversation over multilingual signage in Malaysia. Last year, former prime minister Mahathir Mohamad complained on social media that shopping centres in Kuala Lumpur featured “big” Chinese characters with no Malay wording, saying that “suddenly I felt I was in China”.
He questioned whether the country had “become a part of China”, sparking heated online exchanges in which many Malaysians accused him of politicising the issue and noted that the signs were aimed at tourists rather than signalling any erosion of national identity.

data.title: What to see at Hong Kong Palace Museum’s ancient Egypt exhibition – 5 of the best exhibits
data.description:
From a sculpture of a nine-year-old pharaoh to cat mummies and an ancient tablet comparable to the Rosetta Stone, a new Hong Kong Palace Museum exhibition sheds light on ancient Egyptian civilisation.
Opening November 20 and running until August 31, 2026, “Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums” showcases 250 artefacts, many loaned from the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, with some having never been removed from Egyptian soil before this tour.
Beyond the national treasures, the exhibition – which marks the 70th anniversary of China-Egypt diplomatic relations next year – will also feature more than 10 multimedia installations, a series of public talks, workshops and guided tours.
“This exhibition is more than a display. It is a dialogue between past and present, between Egypt and the world, and between two great cultures united by reverence for timeless beauty and human creativity,” says Dr Mohamed Ismail Khaled, secretary general of the Supreme Council of Antiquities (SCA) in Egypt.

Here are five unmissable highlights:
1. Colossal statue of Tutankhamun in quartzite
The star of the show is the colossal statue of Tutankhamun in quartzite.
The boy king, who ascended to the throne at age nine and died nine years later, was most celebrated for restoring Egypt’s traditional polytheistic beliefs. His modern fame skyrocketed with the discovery of his tomb in 1922, which remained nearly entirely intact for more than three millennia.
This sculpture was discovered at the Egyptian site of Medinet Habu, within the remnants of a temple to his successors, Ay and Horemheb.

Standing three metres (9.8 feet) tall, the statue has youthful features that are atypical for its symmetrical pharaonic counterparts. It depicts the young pharaoh wearing a traditional nemes headdress, adorned with royal blue and gold stripes and a broad usekh collar. A falcon-headed dagger is tucked into the waistband of his royal kilt, also known as a shendyt.
Notably, his throne name, inscribed on the belt clip, appears to have been usurped with the cartouche or symbol of Horemheb. The false beard is mostly missing, though its outline remains clear.
The display is particularly eye-catching for its implementation of a special projection-mapping technology, which digitally restores the damaged portions. The light projection reveals what appears to be an intact sculpture, with a reconstructed face and limbs, and vibrant blue and gold paint.
The statue can be found in the second section dedicated to the pharaoh, titled “The World of Tutankhamun”.
2. Mummies of animals, specifically cats and cattle

The mummification of animals was a widespread practice in ancient Egypt, embedded in a religious world view that blurred the boundaries between the human, the divine and the natural world.
During the Ptolemaic period (332BC-30BC), worship for the feline goddess Bastet – often depicted as a fierce lioness deity of the home, fertility and benevolence – reached its peak. Ancient Egyptians offered mummified cats as gifts to seek the favour of the goddess.
The cat mummies on display vary in presentation. X-rays reveal that while some mummies contain complete cat skeletons, others hold only fragmentary bones or are stuffed with other materials, such as reeds and linen.
Differences were also found in burial methods. Some cats were placed in ornate coffins or bronze statues, while others were wrapped in linen and buried in collective, stacked layers.
Alongside them is a carefully crafted calf mummy and cartonnage, or mummy case, constructed over a wooden frame, which also served a religious purpose as it is associated with the goddess Isis, commonly known as the mother of the divine Apis bull. Calves were seen as a living incarnation of the deity and lived lavishly inside temples.

X-ray analysis confirms it contains the partial remains of a 13- to 16-month-old calf, with a bovine skull placed in the head and other bones within the body. This cartonnage was likely used as a funeral object, either as a coffin lid or a sacred effigy in processions.
The pieces are located in the exhibition’s third section, “The Secrets of Saqqara”.
3. Kneeling Hatshepsut statue

Hatshepsut was one of the most successful pharaohs of the 18th dynasty (1550BC-1292BC) and one of the few female rulers who altered ancient Egyptian history.
What makes this statue so extraordinary is its deliberate blending of gender symbols: Hatshepsut is depicted with a bare masculine chest and wearing traditional male pharaonic regalia, complete with a shendyt kilt and the ceremonial false beard that symbolised divine kingship.
Her 21-year reign required careful political manoeuvring to legitimise her position as a female ruler in a male-dominated institution, making this interpretation of her role greatly significant and revolutionary.
The figure is on display in the first section, “The Land of the Pharaohs”.
4. Canopic jars used for organ storage

A set of four calcite canopic jars belonging to a noblewoman named Setyerboni offer a glimpse into the funerary practices of ancient Egypt.
Each was designed to hold and protect a specific internal organ removed during the mummification process, which the Egyptians believed was essential for the deceased’s rebirth in the afterlife, necessitating their preservation separate from the body.
Unlike earlier periods, where the organs were sometimes returned to the body cavity, the practice of using complete sets of individual jars became the standard for the elite.
An additional finding is an Imsety canopic jar belonging to Tjuyu, the mother-in-law of Amenhotep III, placed next to the collection.
They are held in the first section of the exhibition, “The Land of the Pharaohs”.
5. Stele with three scripts

A limestone stele with three scripts provides a window into a period of profound cultural and linguistic transition in ancient Egypt.
Dating from the Roman period, this stone slab features inscriptions in three distinct writing systems: traditional Egyptian hieroglyphs, the cursive Egyptian Demotic script and ancient Greek.
Beyond its linguistic utility, the stele stands as a powerful symbol of cultural synthesis and political pragmatism. Its very existence illustrates how the foreign Ptolemaic dynasty sought to legitimise its rule by engaging with and adopting Egyptian religious and bureaucratic traditions.
Like the Rosetta Stone, a well-known ancient Egyptian stele, this is another example of the continuity of Egyptian civilisation even under a new, external power.
The stone is placed in the fourth section, “Ancient Egypt and the World”.
“Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums”, Hong Kong Palace Museum, West Kowloon Cultural District, Tsim Sha Tsui, November 20, 2025, to August 31, 2026. Detailed programme and ticketing information can be found on the Hong Kong Palace Museum website.

data.title: Tokyo government ordered to pay US$247,970 in damages in Nepal man’s death
data.description:
A court in Japan has ordered the Tokyo metropolitan government to pay 39 million yen (US$247,970) in damages to the family of a Nepalese man who died after being excessively restrained by police eight years ago.
Arjun Bahadur Singh was arrested in 2017 on suspicion of carrying a credit card that did not belong to him.
Police later kept the 39-year-old in a detention room, tying a belt and rope around his wrists and knees for about two hours after he became violent, the Mainichi newspaper reported.
Singh lost consciousness during questioning at the Tokyo District Public Prosecutors Office. He was subsequently taken to a hospital, where he was pronounced dead.
Seeking a 61.8 million yen payout from the local and central governments, his family approached the Tokyo District Court in 2023, which found that an unreasonable level of force used by police led to the man’s death.
According to the Asahi newspaper, the court also ruled that officers acted illegally by delaying his transfer to hospital, but awarded only 1 million yen to Singh’s dependents, citing Nepal’s compensation laws related to similar cases.

The High Court, however, reversed the judgment on Wednesday and increased the compensation to 39 million yen.
“Legal systems vary across countries, and detailed comparisons are not easy. Such restrictions could lead to unreasonable or harmful consequences from a human rights protection standpoint,” Judge Maki Aizawa said.
Both the courts said the federal government was not responsible for Singh’s death.
“I pray that no one else ever again experiences the pain that I have endured,” the victim’s wife said via her lawyer, Ryutaro Ogawa.
Ogawa hailed the verdict and told the Asahi: “The High Court delivered a fair judgment, compared to the district court’s baseless decision to impose a cap not provided for in the law.”
Police said they would study the ruling before considering their next step.
Japan’s handling of foreigners at detention centres was thrust into the spotlight following the 2021 death of a Sri Lankan woman at an immigration facility in Nagoya.
Wishma Sandamali’s family also filed a lawsuit demanding compensation from the national government, saying she died because officials failed to provide her with medical care when she became sick.
In 2023, the Immigration Services Agency of Japan said 18 foreign nationals had died in detention facilities across the country since 2007.

data.title: US boycott and China-Japan rift loom large as leaders gather for G20 summit
data.description:
Leaders from the world’s wealthiest and largest economies will gather in Johannesburg for the Group of 20 summit this weekend, as geopolitical friction threatens to derail the event.
There are tensions over Washington’s unprecedented total boycott of the summit, and a diplomatic rift between Beijing and Tokyo is also likely to loom large after China ruled out a one-on-one leaders’ meeting with Japan.
Beijing declined a meeting on the sidelines of the G20 summit – which runs from Saturday to Sunday – in response to recent remarks by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.
In parliament on November 7, Takaichi said that if Beijing took military action against Taiwan it could be deemed a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan, potentially allowing Tokyo to exercise its right to collective self-defence and to deploy its armed forces alongside the US in the island region and surrounding waters to defend its vital shipping lanes.


The dispute between China and Japan could threaten consensus on key issues on the table in Johannesburg, according to observers. They said the tensions were also likely to disrupt traditional high-level “corridor diplomacy” at the gathering, where decision-making is driven by the principle of building consensus.
Gustavo de Carvalho, a senior researcher on African governance and diplomacy at the South African Institute of International Affairs, a think tank in Johannesburg, said if the United States was not in the room and China and Japan were barely talking, any communique from the summit might not be seen as fully credible as a global economic management framework.
In addition, Carvalho said that if a communique were released, it could be expected that Washington might use it as a basis for condemnation and pushback when it holds the G20 presidency next year.
South Africa is expected to hand over the presidency to the US for 2026 at this weekend’s summit, but President Cyril Ramaphosa has said it would be a symbolic handover to an “empty chair”, acknowledging the US boycott.
Carvalho said the China-Japan feud would also have an impact on the summit but it was likely to be “less severe” than the US absence.
“While both are central Asian hubs in global value chains, their current stand-off increases the fact that most G20 discussions are not just economic, but geoeconomic,” Carvalho added.
He said that may require some language to be toned down, but noted that most of the negotiations for the communique had already taken place or were under way, with South Africa having hosted more than 100 G20-related meetings this year.

John Kirton, director of the G20 Research Group at the University of Toronto, did not expect the China-Japan dispute to overshadow the summit.
“[Chinese] Premier Li Qiang will ensure that no declaration or chair’s statement says anything critical of China or supportive of Taiwan, no matter how hard Prime Minister Takaichi would want it to,” Kirton said.
He said Takaichi’s comments and the harsh Chinese response were not unusually serious as they were a matter of words rather than military forces moving into position to attack or defend.
Washington has said it would completely boycott this weekend’s G20 summit, a first in the forum’s history, with President Donald Trump accusing South Africa of persecuting white farmers – a claim its government denies.
The US absence could pave the way for increased influence by the China-led Brics nations and the Global South at the gathering.
On Thursday, Ramaphosa said the US had reversed its decision and that talks were under way regarding sending a delegation to the G20.
However, the White House immediately dismissed the claims as false, confirming that the US would not participate, although an official from its embassy in South Africa would attend the formal handover for the 2026 G20 presidency.
On the agenda, South Africa has prioritised the need to prepare for climate-induced disasters, sustainable debt for low-income nations, financing energy transitions, and ensuring that the rush for critical minerals benefits producers. Trump has rejected South Africa’s agenda for the summit – a move that could critically undermine the entire G20 consensus.
Carvalho at the Johannesburg think tank said the US boycott created a leadership vacuum at the G20, but it was not a blank cheque for Beijing or other powers.
He said Trump’s decision to keep the entire US government away removed a major source of contention and a key driver of the G20, weakening the group’s ability to set global standards on economic governance.
However, a broader range of countries is stepping forward. Carvalho said that for South Africa and the African Union, the summit provided an important space for normative influence – bringing key African issues into a global discussion forum. He gave the examples of inequality, debt sustainability and the cost of capital.
“Even with a diluted presence, these discussions influence not only the current and future G20 discussions, but tend also to spill over into other spaces like UN discussions, for instance,” Carvalho added.
Sarang Shidore, director of the Global South programme at the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft, a Washington-based think tank, said there were significant gaps between the Group of Seven (G7) component of the G20 and the Global South states in the grouping.
“Geopolitical challenges such as Ukraine are less divisive in the grouping than they were earlier, for example at the 2022 summit in Indonesia,” Shidore said.

data.title: CY Leung’s charity moves to 104-year-old Hong Kong site of ‘philanthropic spirit’
data.description:
A Hong Kong charity founded by a former city leader has relocated its headquarters to a 104-year-old historic site at a nominal premium of HK$1,000 (US$128), with the organisation saying it hopes to inherit the philanthropic spirit of the location.
The GX Foundation announced on Friday that it had moved to No 4 Hospital Road in Mid-Levels, with the site built in response to a plague outbreak that started in the late 19th century, before serving as a cleaners’ residence and later a shelter for street sleepers.
“This building … has its character, its history and is quite prominent in the neighbourhood. And more importantly, it has its spirit of philanthropy behind it,” the foundation’s chairman, former chief executive Leung Chun-ying, said.
“We’d like to inherit and extend this spirit through our work, and hopefully spread this message to others in Hong Kong, particularly young students.”
The site in Sai Ying Pun comprises a five-storey main building and a single-storey annexe, surrounded by medical institutions and former facilities turned into heritage sites, such as the Government Civil Hospital, the city’s first public hospital.
Boasting an area of about 1,047 square metres, the site also enjoys grade two historic status under the city’s three-tier system, meaning it is deemed to have special merit and should be “selectively preserved”.
The foundation said the construction of the building was prompted by a bubonic plague outbreak that started in the 1890s and returned annually for more than three decades. Over 20,000 cases were reported, with a mortality rate of about 90 per cent.

The site was completed in 1921 before being used as cleaners’ quarters until the 1990s, when the Social Welfare Department turned it into a shelter for the homeless.
In 2007, it became an office building of the Centre for Food Safety and was finally granted to the foundation in July this year by city leader John Lee Ka-chiu and the Executive Council at a nominal premium of HK$1,000 under the arrangement of a private treaty.
According to the Lands Department, land in the city can be granted by way of private treaty grant for a specified use in justified circumstances to comply with approved government policies and to meet Hong Kong’s economic, social and community needs.
Such direct land grants are subject to stringent policy scrutiny and are considered to be in the public interest.
Leung, who is also a vice-chairman of the national committee of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference, the country’s top political advisory body, said the building was a convenient place for the charity to get in touch with the local community, adding that the foundation would hold more outreach events at the site.
The first open house event will be held on Sunday.
Professor Emily Chan Ying-yang, the foundation’s CEO, said such events would be held at least twice a year, while groups could also make appointments to visit.
The foundation was established by Leung in 2018 and focuses on humanitarian assistance in countries along the Belt and Road Initiative, such as treating cataract blindness, combating dengue fever and improving water safety.
Its initiatives already extend to 10 countries, including Honduras, Fiji, Laos, Cambodia and Djibouti.
Looking ahead, Leung said the charity was looking at how to expand its services beyond these countries.
“Now that we have earned a reputation globally and we have received frequent requests to expand our work to other countries,” he said.

data.title: Malaysian rapper Namewee’s job hunt amid Iris Hsieh death probe sparks outrage
data.description:
Malaysian rapper Namewee has been criticised by Taiwanese model Sprite Fang for seeking part-time jobs amid ongoing investigations into the death of social media influencer Iris Hsieh, Oriental Daily reported.
In a social media post on Wednesday, Fang, 42, wrote: “You don’t have money, but at least you still have your life. You can keep writing music to generate income. However, Iris had money but is no longer alive to spend it.”
Fang urged Namewee, whose real name is Wee Meng Chee, to take responsibility for her close friend’s death and to show more sincerity moving forward.
“Can you pay your respects to Iris with the utmost sincerity? Death itself isn’t frightening. What’s most frightening is being forgotten by everyone,” she wrote.
On Monday, Namewee, 42, announced on social media that he was looking for part-time jobs, as all of his work through the end of 2026 has been cancelled.
He added that he was looking for sponsors for his coming Lunar New Year song after the previous sponsor withdrew reportedly due to the rapper’s arrest.
Namewee surrendered himself to authorities on November 5, pledging full cooperation in the investigation of Hsieh’s death.

The case was reclassified as murder after initially being treated as a sudden death.
Investigations showed that Namewee was the last person seen with Hsieh in a Kuala Lumpur hotel room on October 22.
On November 12, Attorney General Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar stated that there was no evidence linking Namewee to Hsieh’s death, and he would be released on police bail.
Namewee later said in a statement that he had done everything he could to save Hsieh but to no avail.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Cryptocurrency market extends slide as bitcoin falls below US$86,000
data.description:
The cryptocurrency market extended a more than month-long retreat during Asia trading on Friday.
Market bellwether bitcoin dropped as much as 2.1 per cent to reach below US$86,000 for the first time since April, as the market struggled to find new buyers and the momentum that had supported prices earlier this year evaporated.
The pullback comes after weeks of unwinding among fast-moving traders and lingering positioning from October’s record run-up, which has left the market more vulnerable to selling pressure and sharp swings.
Bitcoin was trading at US$85,474 at 10.59am in Singapore on Friday.
“Crypto is suffering from heavy selling by whales who follow the four-year cycle narrative, and this is typically the point in that cycle where prices fall,” said James Butterfill, head of research at European asset manager CoinShares.
“While we don’t subscribe to this view from a fundamentals perspective, it has become somewhat self-fulfilling, with large holders selling more than US$20 billion since September.”
Stocks had surged on a fresh wave of artificial intelligence exuberance following positive results from Nvidia Corp, only to see those gains evaporate.

Concerns about lofty AI valuations and doubts over the US Federal Reserve’s ability to cut rates in December have led to volatility on Wall Street.
Cryptocurrencies remain trapped in a self-contained purge of leverage and fading retail demand – a cross-asset split that has deepened since early October.
The slump has options traders eyeing the US$85,000 threshold. Downside protection around that level has received the highest demand, followed by US$82,000, according to options contracts on Coinbase-owned crypto exchange Deribit.
“Crypto’s implosion has stripped away one of the market’s main speculative outlets,” said Brendan Fagan, macro strategist at Bloomberg. “In this environment, crypto is no longer the canary in the coal mine – it’s the coal mine itself, collapsing under the weight of its own leverage.”
Bitcoin’s latest slide cannot be separated from October’s violent liquidation cascade, when more than US$19 billion in leveraged positions were forced out in a single session.
That shock broke momentum and hollowed out liquidity across major venues. Order books never fully rebuilt, leaving prices hypersensitive to modest flows – a fragility that continues to define every downdraft.
At the same time, economic angst has added to Wall Street jitters.
“Uncertainty lingers over how the Federal Reserve will set policy in the current data vacuum – and that uncertainty, more than anything else, is suppressing investors’ willingness to take on risk which is most evident in riskier assets,” said Jake Ostrovskis, head over-the-counter trading at algorithmic trading firm Wintermute.

data.title: How China-Japan spat is disrupting travel and what it means for Hong Kong
data.description:
Rising tensions between China and Japan have injected uncertainty into the tourism sector, with both mainland and Hong Kong authorities cautioning against travel to the country.
Nearly half a million air tickets from the mainland to Japan were cancelled, while a Hong Kong tour operator also reported a decline in inquiries about travel there. Some Hong Kong airlines have offered flexible arrangements for passengers wishing to change their holiday plans in Japan.
The Post reviews developments and examines what may lie ahead for the industry and holidaymakers.
1. What caused the row?
The recent tensions stemmed from comments by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi on November 7, when she suggested the country might consider military action if China attempted to invade or blockade Taiwan.
Beijing viewed these remarks as a serious challenge to its red line regarding Taiwan and Japan’s military responses.
China’s foreign ministry issued a warning on Friday last week, urging its people to avoid travelling to Japan due to “continued deterioration” in their safety there.

2. How did Hong Kong respond?
A day after Beijing warned against travelling to Japan, Hong Kong’s Security Bureau urged residents on November 15 to exercise caution and prioritise personal safety in the country, citing a rise in attacks against Chinese citizens.
The government also reminded Hong Kong residents planning to visit Japan or already there to pay attention to local announcements and notices from the Chinese embassy or consulate general regarding the latest situation or consular services.
The bureau previously issued amber travel warnings only for parts near Japan’s Fukushima nuclear power plant, but the alert had not been upgraded.
The city’s outbound travel warnings follow a three-tier system. The most severe warning is black, which means avoid all travel, followed by red and amber.
3. Are local airlines giving full refunds for Japan trips?
About 491,000 air tickets from the mainland to Japan were cancelled within a short period after carriers swiftly announced free refunds or itinerary changes for flights booked through December 31, according to a veteran aviation analyst.
But Hong Kong carriers appeared to have opted for a “flexible” approach on cancellations.
As of Thursday, Greater Bay Airlines remained the first and only Hong Kong carrier to explicitly offer refunds for Japan-bound flights following Beijing’s travel warning to Chinese citizens.
It said it would offer flexible arrangements to customers who bought tickets on or before November 15 for departures to Japan on or before December 31.
Cathay Pacific Airways, the city’s flag carrier, and its budget arm HK Express said they would provide flexible arrangements for passengers.

4. Will the city face a wave of travel cancellations?
No obvious trend has emerged so far, though one agency reported a drop in inquiries.
Yuen Chun-ning, CEO of travel agency WWPKG, said on Thursday that inquiries about Japan tours had fallen by 20 to 30 per cent and some customers had asked whether they could withdraw.
Yuen said callers were asking whether they could change destinations or dates, or even cancel altogether – what handling fees would apply and how they could get a full refund.
But he stressed that unless the Security Bureau raised its outbound travel alert to red or higher, or flights were cancelled, all tours would depart as scheduled.
He added that any withdrawals from tour packages would still result in cancellation fees.
Lau Siu-kai, a consultant for the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said he believed a small proportion of Hong Kong travellers might be worried about going, but most would proceed with their plans.
He did not expect large-scale withdrawals from tours or mass ticket cancellations unless major developments occurred. He added that such cancellations would only likely happen in the event of physical attacks or unfriendly treatment from Japanese people, particularly those with right-wing political leanings, against mainland Chinese or Hong Kong tourists.
